figures.caption: Figure 2: Normality of pseudo-noise across timesteps. Heat-maps show pass (green) or fail (red) at α = 0.05 for (top) Shapiro–Wilk, (middle) Anderson–Darling, and (bottom) Kolmogorov–Smirnov tests. Rows correspond to the four datasets, spiral, circle, Gaussian blob, CIFAR-10. With only a handful of isolated rejections, as well as extremely low SNR ratio, the pseudo-noise is effectively Gaussian at almost every t, supporting the normality assumption.

Table 2: Hyperparameters used for CIFAR-10 and ImageNet 64x64 experiments. Baseline represents the CM that learns the ∅ distribution, which majorly follows ECT. Both Naive and Random JFDL in Tab. 1 share the same training hyperparameters, where we summarize under the JFDL column. The CIFAR-10 experiments has an additional preliminary column that concludes the settings in Fig. 3.

Figure 3: Preliminary results tuning LJFDL only. CIFAR-10 samples from Naive JFDL (top left) v.s. Random JFDL (bottom left). FID w.r.t. ω plot (right) reflects the stronger guidance effect from Random JFDL compared to Naive, causing the FID to diverge faster.

Figure 4: FID to guidance strength progression. The red dotted line is the FID of the initial unguided ECT, the orange curve and blue curve represents the FID progression w.r.t ω for Naive and Random JFDL respectively.

Figure 6: Three synthetic 2D toy distributions used for normality verification experiments, spiral (left), circle (middle), and Gaussian blob (right). We train a DM that learns the class and ∅-class ODE. We use the DM as an ODE solver to reconstruct the pseudo-noise (10) for any t, and test whether the pseudo-noise passes the normality test.
Figure 5: ImageNet 64x64 sample results. The classes shown are "hotdog" and "jay", generated by ECT + Naive JFDL. Rows are guidance strength. Columns are sampling steps.

Figure 1: Comparison of class v.s. ∅-conditioned ODE solutions and their marginal distributions. Each row corresponds to a different 2-D toy dataset, spiral, circle, Gaussian blob. We choose three different time steps, σt = 0.002, 0.207, 23.771 (from left to right), and compare the distribution of a hybrid flow p(y∅,t 0 ) (left) with the class distribution p(xc0|c) (right) separated by dotted lines. In Prop. 1, we state that the marginal of the two are the same.
